reason: The Hebrew people (Israelites) are portrayed as God's chosen people who are miraculously saved and divinely protected. Despite displaying human weakness—fear, doubt, and complaint when facing Pharaoh's army—they are ultimately depicted as the recipients of God's extraordinary intervention. The narrative culminates with them safely delivered through the parted sea, while their Egyptian pursuers are destroyed. Importantly, the passage concludes with the Israelites fearing (revering) God and believing in both the LORD and Moses, establishing them as people worthy of divine salvation and favor. This fundamental framing as the elect recipients of God's redemptive power constitutes a favorable portrayal, even though the text honestly depicts their momentary loss of faith and their initial complaints.
